Two bugs: (a) `getLedCircles` was role-EXCLUSIVE — it ran the `host_id` query only when the caller's COMMUNITY rung was exactly `'host'`, the guide branch only for `'guide'`, etc. So a leader whose rung was anything else (a mentor, or a staffer/janitor) who DIRECTLY hosted a circle saw "Circles you host: 0" — hosting is a per-circle fact, not a rung. (b) `requireLeadFloor` gated at `community_role >= host`, so a sub-host-rung host (or a staffer who hosts) was redirected from their own leadership home. The page was also a hand-built `DashboardTemplate`, not the module engine.

**Decision.**
1. **`getLedCircles(profileId)` is additive + cached.** It always unions circles the caller hosts (`circles.host_id`), circles in hubs they guide (`hubs.guide_id`), and circles in hubs under nexuses they mentor (`nexuses.mentor_id`), de-duped, ordered by name, wrapped in React `cache()` so multiple dashboard blocks share one read. The `role` parameter is dropped (callers updated).
2. **`requireLeadFloor` admits host+ OR staff OR anyone who actually leads something** — a cheap `host_id`/`guide_id`/`mentor_id` existence check, run only for non-host non-staff callers. (`crew` is a deprecated no-op rung, so gating literally on "crew and up" wasn't meaningful; "anyone who adopted a leadership role" is implemented as "leads anything".)
3. **`/lead` is module-driven:** `AdminTemplate` + `<PageModules route="/lead">` with self-fetching, caller-scoped blocks (stats · what-needs-you · circles · co-leaders · networks · events · dispatches · journeys · recognition · tools — 10 blocks), each self-hiding when empty, arrangeable from Settings → Layout. New blocks verified against real schema: `lead-coleaders` (`memberships.volunteer_role`), `lead-dispatches` (`dispatches` `audience_scope='circle'` + `posts` `post_type='announcement'`), `lead-recognition` (`profiles.current_streak >= 7` + `referred_by_profile_id`). No fabricated metrics.

**Rationale.** Leadership is the union of every way you steward, independent of rung; the old role-gated read was the source of the "0 circles" bug, and the floor followed the same flawed assumption. Module-driving `/lead` aligns it with the rest of the app and makes every block arrangeable + per-role gateable.

**Consequences.** `app/(main)/lead/load-led-circles.ts` (additive + cached), `lib/admin/guard.ts` (floor), `components/widgets/lead/*` (10 blocks), `lib/widgets/{modules,registry,module-routes}` carry the `/lead` set, `crew-tasks` caller updated to the new signature. No migration. `tsc` 0 / `eslint` / `pnpm build` green. Follow-up available: `lead-dispatches` is a read-only feed; a full compose/inbox messaging hub is a larger surface.

## ADR-404: A uniform column gap (lg:gap-10) across the app shell, admin rail, and detail grids

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-25). The inter-column gap is standardized at `lg:gap-10` (40px) on large screens so every user page's columns breathe uniformly, matching the admin workspace.

**Context.** Member pages read "tight" between the center content and the right rail versus the admin workspace. The app-shell content row used `gap-8` (32px) uniformly, and the admin info-rail reached its 32px via a `w-64` rail inside a `w-72` column — so the gaps were actually equal, but the spacing felt cramped and the owner wanted more breathing room, uniform across user pages. (On detail pages the "tight" reading is compounded by a separate issue: a page can render its own inner rail AND the global member rail, so two right columns sit side by side.)

**Decision.** Standardize the column gap at `lg:gap-10` (40px), applied so left-menu↔content and content↔rail stay equal:
1. The app-shell content row: `gap-8` → `gap-8 lg:gap-10` (`components/layout/app-shell.tsx`).
2. The admin info-rail column buffer widened to match: `AdminRailDrawerColumn` `xl:w-72` → `xl:w-[18.5rem]` (16rem rail + 2.5rem gap), so admin content↔rail equals content↔menu at the new gap.
3. Detail-page inner grids beside the shell rail follow suit: the circle page's `grid-cols-3` body uses `lg:gap-10`.

**Rationale.** A single column-gap token keeps every user page's columns visually uniform and breathing, matching the admin reference. Presentational only (Tailwind classes), no behavior change.

**Consequences.** `components/layout/app-shell.tsx`, `components/admin/admin-rail-drawer-column.tsx`, `app/(main)/circles/[slug]/page.tsx`. The underlying double-rail on detail pages (an inner rail plus the global member rail) is a separate follow-up — the gap is now 40px but two right columns can still co-exist. No migration.

## ADR-405: Circle page rail layout — operator default + host override, no new schema

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-25). The circle detail page's right-rail layout (order + visibility of its blocks) is controlled at two levels: a network-wide operator default and a per-circle host override, resolved host → operator → coded default.

**Context.** The circle page right rail already reordered from `circles.sidebar_order` (host-chosen, order-only, ADR-181), but the editor was stale (5 of ~9 blocks, no show/hide) and there was no operator-level default. The owner wanted control of the individual circle page at both levels: a network default they set as operator, and a per-circle override each host can set.

**Decision.** A layout is `{ order: string[], hidden: string[] }`. Resolution (`lib/circles/rail-layout.ts` `resolveRailOrder`) is **host override ?? operator default ?? coded default**, with any block that actually built but the layout doesn't name appended (visible) so a newly added rail block never disappears. One block manifest (`RAIL_BLOCKS`) is the single source of truth for keys + labels, shared by the render and both editors.
1. **Host override** rides on the existing `circles.sidebar_order` (jsonb), widened from `string[]` to the `{order,hidden}` object; `coerceLayout` reads both shapes, so legacy rows keep working. Saved via the re-gated `saveSidebarOrder` (circle.editSettings).
2. **Operator default** is a single `platform_settings` row (key `circle_rail_layout`, JSON), read by `getOperatorRailLayout` and written by the janitor-only `saveCircleRailDefault`. Mounted on `/admin/circles` for janitors.
3. One shared client editor (`RailLayoutEditor`, drag-reorder + eye toggle) backs both surfaces; the old `SidebarWidgetEditor` is removed.

**Rationale.** Reusing `sidebar_order` (jsonb) + `platform_settings` means **no migration** and no new table — the lowest-risk path to two-level control. Capability gating still wins: the render only orders blocks that built for the viewer, so a layout can't surface a manager-only block to a visitor. The pure `rail-layout.ts` (type-only import from `lib/pillars`-style pattern) keeps server code out of the client editors.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/circles/rail-layout.ts`, `lib/circles/rail-layout-store.ts`, `components/circles/rail-layout-editor.tsx`, `app/(main)/admin/circles/layout-actions.ts`. Changed: `app/(main)/circles/[slug]/page.tsx` (render), `app/(main)/circles/admin-actions.ts` (`saveSidebarOrder` now takes `{order,hidden}`), `components/admin/modules/circle-rail-module.tsx`, `app/(main)/admin/circles/page.tsx`. Removed: `components/circles/sidebar-widget-editor.tsx`. No migration.

## ADR-406: Circle detail page joins the page-settings module engine (supersedes ADR-405)

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-25). The individual circle page (`/circles/<slug>`) now renders its body through the per-route module-assignment engine (`<PageModules>`, ADR-270/271/272/294) — the SAME "Layout" system as `/practices` and `/practices/<id>`. The bespoke circle rail-layout system from ADR-405 is removed.

**Context.** ADR-405 built a circle-specific rail reorder + operator default on `circles.sidebar_order` + `platform_settings`. The owner's intent was the actual page-settings Layout app (template picker + HEADER/MAIN/SIDE module areas, per-module show/hide, reorder, and who-sees-it) shown on `/practices` — not a bespoke rail-only box. ADR-405 was the wrong mechanism.

**Decision.** Convert the circle body (the feed + the info-rail blocks) to nine self-fetching layout modules and render them with `<PageModules route={`/circles/${slug}`}>`, shared across every circle via the `/circles/*` section scope.
1. **Modules** (`components/widgets/circles/*`): `circle-feed`, `circle-members`, `circle-health`, `circle-momentum`, `circle-practice`, `circle-events`, `circle-map`, `circle-invite`, `circle-journey-run` — registered in `lib/widgets/modules.ts` (`CIRCLE_DETAIL_MODULE_IDS` at `/circles/*`) + bound in `registry.tsx`. The page header (cover · title · badges · Join/Settings) stays fixed.
2. **Per-entity context** (`lib/circles/active-circle.ts`): the page resolves all per-viewer data ONCE and stamps a request-scoped `CircleDetailContext` (a `React.cache` holder, mirroring `lib/spaces/active-space.ts`); each zero-prop module reads it back — no re-fetch, no prop-drilling. Per-circle capability gating stays inside each module (a manager-only block returns null for a visitor regardless of the engine's role gate).
3. **Two-column default** (`lib/page-settings/default-layouts.ts`): a per-route DEFAULT layout (`/circles/*` → `main-side`, feed in MAIN, rail in SIDE) that BOTH the renderer (`loadLayoutForRoute`) and the editor (`getPageLayoutForEditor`) fall back to when nothing is saved, so the page reads right out of the box and the editor opens matching it.
4. **Editor surface**: `/circles/<slug>` is registered as a `MODULE_SECTION`, so `isModuleRoute` is true and the on-page Settings shows the standard `LayoutEditor` — mounted in the circle settings drawer, OPERATOR-gated (the `/circles/*` layout is network-wide).

**Rationale.** One engine for every arrangeable page: operators arrange the circle page exactly like Practices, with the template picker / areas / who-sees-it they already know. The `active-circle` holder keeps the conversion to thin presentational modules (no duplicate fetching). The per-route default avoids a migration while still giving a good two-column page with no operator action.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/circles/active-circle.ts`, `lib/circles/detail-types.ts`, `components/widgets/circles/*` (9), `lib/page-settings/default-layouts.ts`. Changed: `app/(main)/circles/[slug]/page.tsx` (stamps context + `<PageModules>`), `lib/widgets/modules.ts` / `registry.tsx` / `module-routes.ts`, `lib/page-settings/store.ts` + `actions.ts` (default fallback), `components/layout/settings-drawer.tsx` (Layout editor on circles), `app/(main)/circles/admin-actions.ts` + `app/(main)/admin/circles/page.tsx` (rail-layout removed). Removed (ADR-405): `lib/circles/rail-layout.ts`, `lib/circles/rail-layout-store.ts`, `components/circles/rail-layout-editor.tsx`, `components/admin/modules/circle-rail-module.tsx`, `app/(main)/admin/circles/layout-actions.ts`. No migration (`circles.sidebar_order` is now unused — left in place; a later migration may drop it). Per-host rail reordering is dropped: layout is operator-only, matching the page-settings model.

## ADR-407: A movable circle Page-text block (network default + per-circle override), reusing `circles.sidebar_order`

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-25). Circles gain a free rich-text block (`circle-text`) that an operator can place ANYWHERE on the circle page through the same Layout editor (ADR-406) — header, MAIN, or the info-rail. Its copy resolves per-circle override → network default.

**Context.** The owner asked for "a text field that moves around anywhere on the page" with "formatting options," scoped (their answer) to "one global default + per-circle override." The circle page is already module-driven (ADR-406), so a movable block is just one more layout module; what it needed was a content layer and two small editors.

**Decision.**
1. **One more layout module** — `circle-text` is added to `CIRCLE_DETAIL_MODULE_IDS`/`registry.tsx` and leads the (empty-by-default) `header` slot in the `/circles/*` default layout, so operators move it with the blocks they already arrange. The RSC (`components/widgets/circles/circle-text.tsx`) reads the resolved copy from the request-scoped `CircleDetailContext` (`layoutText`) and renders it through the existing safe markdown subset (`richParagraphs`: `**bold**` · `*italic*` · `[label](/path)`, no `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`). Empty copy → renders nothing, so an unset block never leaves a hole.
2. **Two content levels** (`lib/circles/circle-text.ts` read helpers + `circle-text-actions.ts` `'use server'` actions): the NETWORK DEFAULT is a `platform_settings` row (`circle_layout_text`, janitor-set); the PER-CIRCLE OVERRIDE reuses `circles.sidebar_order` — the jsonb column ADR-406 left unused — as `{ text }`. `resolveCircleText` returns the override, else the default. No migration.
3. **Two editors, each re-gated server-side.** Per-circle: a new dock module `circle.text` (registry + `module-map`, `components/admin/modules/circle-text-module.tsx`) in the circle Settings drawer, gated by `circle.editSettings` (host + operators). Network default: a janitor-only section on `/admin/circles` (`circle-text-default-editor.tsx`). The split mirrors ADR-405's "operator default + host override," but as content, not layout — layout placement stays operator-only via ADR-406's editor.

**Why split `circle-text.ts` from `circle-text-actions.ts`.** A `'use server'` module may export only async actions; the pure parser `circleTextOverride` (sync) and the server-side read helpers belong in a plain module the RSC page imports, while the client editors import the actions.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/circles/circle-text.ts`, `lib/circles/circle-text-actions.ts`, `components/widgets/circles/circle-text.tsx`, `components/admin/modules/circle-text-module.tsx`, `app/(main)/admin/circles/circle-text-default-editor.tsx`. Changed: `lib/widgets/modules.ts` + `registry.tsx` (register `circle-text`), `lib/page-settings/default-layouts.ts` (header slot), `lib/circles/active-circle.ts` + `detail-types.ts` (`layoutText` + `sidebar_order`), `app/(main)/circles/[slug]/page.tsx` (resolve + stamp), `lib/admin/modules/registry.ts` + `components/admin/modules/module-map.tsx` (`circle.text` dock module), `app/(main)/admin/circles/page.tsx` (default editor). Updates ADR-406's note that `sidebar_order` is unused: it now holds the per-circle text override (so a future "drop the column" migration must not run).

---

## ADR-408: Mindless ambient loops — seamless in the browser, no asset pipeline

**Status:** Accepted · `lib/on-air-ambient.ts` + `AMBIENT_TRACKS` in `lib/on-air.ts` + the Ambient row in `components/on-air/session.tsx`; files in `public/tracks/`. Closes the last unscheduled On Air nicety (docs/ON-AIR.md, "optional ambient audio"), shipped as P17.
**Context:** Members wanted an optional soft background for a Be Still sit. The source tracks are licensed Epidemic Sound MP3s that do not loop cleanly: a plain `<audio loop>` clicks at the seam — MP3 encoder padding leaves a few ms of silence at the ends, and a track's last sample rarely lines up with its first. An offline ffmpeg pre-processing step (crossfade-to-self → committed loop files) was prototyped and rejected: it adds a toolchain, a build step to remember for every new track, and reprocessed binaries in git history.
**Decision:** Do the seamlessness in code, matching the bell's "pure browser audio, no asset pipeline" stance (`lib/timer-audio.ts`). The player fetches + `decodeAudioData`s the MP3 once, builds a **crossfade-to-self loop buffer** in JS (the tail blended back over the head so the wrap lands on the same audio on both sides), and loops THAT on an `AudioBufferSourceNode` — sample-accurate, gapless. Volume rides a `GainNode`: a 3s fade-in at the start of the sit, a duck to silence on pause, a lift on resume, a fade-out at the end. It reuses the bell's shared, gesture-unlocked `AudioContext`. Tracks are a static registry (`AMBIENT_TRACKS`) over `/public/tracks/*.mp3`; the choice persists in `profiles.meta.onAir` (loaded by `lib/on-air/session-data.ts`, saved by `completeSession`) and is mirrored into the crash-recovery record so a recovered sit restores its ambience. Scoped to Be Still; Get Moving is a candidate follow-up. Default off; hidden for Just Log.
**Consequences:** Any MP3 dropped into `public/tracks/` plus one registry line loops seamlessly with **no build step**. Cost is runtime memory: a decoded ambient track is large (tens of MB), so the player keeps only the most-recently-decoded buffer (one ever plays at a time). The whole path is wrapped like the bell — a missing or undecodable file just means no ambience, never a blocked sit. The crossfade is a constant-sum linear blend tuned for correlated material (field recordings, pads); a track deliberately engineered *not* to loop will still sound natural but is not guaranteed click-perfect.

---

## ADR-409: Event detail page joins the page-settings module engine (mirrors ADR-406), via a partial conversion

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). The event detail page (`/events/<slug>`) renders its POST AREA through the per-route module-assignment engine (`<PageModules>`, ADR-270/294/406) — the same Layout system as the circle page and `/practices`. The fixed header, the RSVP/ticket Join aside, and the mobile action bar stay in the page.

**Context.** The owner asked to bring Events to the circle page's bar: operator-arrangeable body + an "Edit event" entry, mirroring Circles. Unlike the circle page, the event page's Join column (RSVP states, capacity/waitlist, ticket tiers, payouts, check-in) is deeply interwoven and is REUSED in the mobile bottom bar, so modularizing it would be high-risk for little gain.

**Decision — a PARTIAL conversion.** Only the post-area content becomes modules; the action-critical chrome stays fixed.
1. **Request-scoped context** (`lib/events/active-event.ts`, mirroring `active-circle.ts`): the page resolves all per-viewer data ONCE and stamps an `EventDetailContext` (a `React.cache` holder); each zero-prop module reads it back. The fixed header + Join aside keep reading the page's locals directly.
2. **Seven post-area modules** (`components/widgets/events/*`): `event-description`, `event-poster-details`, `event-cohosts`, `event-sales`, `event-dispatch`, `event-activity`, `event-recap` — each self-gates to null. Registered in `modules.ts` (`EVENT_DETAIL_MODULE_IDS` at `/events/*`) + `registry.tsx`. Built by a 7-way agent fan-out, each agent verifying the wrapped component's props.
3. **Single-column default + editor gate**: `default-layouts.ts` `/events/*` → `single` template, the seven in `main`. `isEventDetailRoute` (module-routes.ts) marks ONLY `/events/<slug>` a module route — NOT the bespoke `/events/new`, `/scan`, `/drafts` siblings — so the Layout editor (operator-only, in the event Settings drawer) shows only on a real event. An `EditEventButton` opens that drawer from the header, mirroring "Edit Circle".

**Why keep the global rail.** The audit flagged a "double-rail trap" and proposed `railFor('/events/<slug>') → 'none'`. REJECTED: it contradicts the owner directive (2026-06-20, "the right rail shows on every page") and how the circle page actually shipped — circles keep the GLOBAL community rail beside their in-body side column. To genuinely mirror circles, events keep `'global'` too; `page-chrome.ts` is untouched.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/events/active-event.ts`, `components/widgets/events/*` (7), `components/events/edit-event-button.tsx`. Changed: `app/(main)/events/[slug]/page.tsx` (stamp context + `<PageModules>` for the post area; ~100 lines of inline body removed), `lib/widgets/modules.ts` / `registry.tsx` / `module-routes.ts` (`isEventDetailRoute`), `lib/page-settings/default-layouts.ts` (`/events/*`), `components/layout/settings-drawer.tsx` (Layout editor on events). The Join column / mobile bar / cover-title-subtitle header stay hand-built; a future ADR may modularize the Join column once its reuse is refactored.

## ADR-410: A free-tier ticket claim records a normal "going" RSVP

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). Claiming a `free` ticket tier now persists an `event_rsvps` row via the canonical `setRsvpStatus(eventId, 'going')` path, closing the tracked gap where the claim recorded nothing (EVENTS-AUDIT item #3).

**Context.** A host can create a `free` tier on a ticketed event. `createTicketCheckout` returns `{ free: true }` (no money, no Stripe) and, by design, left the caller to record the claim — but `startTicket` never did, so the "Claim ticket" button reloaded and persisted nothing. The claim could be modelled two ways: an `event_tickets` row (host sold-roster, QR check-in, counts against the tier's own `quantity`) or an `event_rsvps` row (event-level capacity/waitlist, first-RSVP gem, confirmation, cancel/withdraw). The two carry different capacity models and are unreconciled.

**Decision.** Record it as a normal **going RSVP** (owner call, 2026-06-26). In `startTicket`, when `createTicketCheckout` returns `free`, call `setRsvpStatus(eventId, 'going')` and return `{ free: true }` unchanged. This reuses the one going-RSVP path (capacity trigger → waitlist when full, gem-once on first RSVP, best-effort confirmation, host validation, idempotent), so a free claim behaves exactly like the RSVP a free event already uses. `createTicketCheckout` still runs first, so its `active` / `member_only` / sold-out inventory gates apply before any RSVP is written.

**Tradeoff (explicit).** A free claim does NOT mint an `event_tickets` row or bump the tier's `sold`, so the tier's own `quantity` is not separately metered against claims — **event capacity governs**. A free claimant therefore gets no QR-ticket/check-in object and isn't in the host's sold-tickets roster; they appear in the RSVP roster. The button stays gated behind `hostPayoutReady` for now (unchanged); ungating free claims from the payout switch is a separate follow-up, since free needs no payout.

**Consequences.** Changed: `app/(main)/events/[slug]/ticket-actions.ts` (import + record the claim). No schema change (reuses `event_rsvps`). EVENTS-AUDIT item #3 and the EVENTS-SYSTEM "still parked" list updated to ✅.

---

## ADR-411: Standardized page header as first-class template props + the entity admin-settings kit

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). The standardized page header (breadcrumb -> cropped hero -> title) is now first-class props on the shared templates, and the in-place entity Settings module is a documented 9-step kit. See PAGE-FRAMEWORK §8.5.

**Context.** The header lockup had drifted into ~4 near-identical hand-composed copies (Circles, Events, Practices, Journeys each rebuilt a banner). Separately, the in-place admin-settings recipe (an entity's cover/identity editor in the page dock) was re-derived by hand each time it was added, with no canonical step list, so each entity's wiring varied subtly. Both are exactly the kind of "structure that should be in the kit, not authored per page" the framework exists to prevent (§8).

**Decision.** Promote the header to template props and write the settings recipe down once.
1. **`IndexTemplate` gains `trail` + `heroImage`** (`components/templates/index-template.tsx`). `trail?: Crumb[]` (`{ href, label }`, exported) renders a `<Breadcrumbs>` at the top of the header; `heroImage?: string | null` renders the STANDARD cropped banner (`h-44 ... object-cover sm:h-56`) only when set. `banner?: React.ReactNode` is demoted to the ESCAPE HATCH for a bespoke header; prefer `trail` + `heroImage`. The page sources the hero from the Settings header image (`getPageHeaderImage`, `lib/page-settings/store.ts`) and/or the page-content hero (`resolvePageContent` `heroImage`).
2. **`DetailTemplate` gains `coverImage`** (`components/templates/detail-template.tsx`), the symmetric twin of `heroImage`: a string renders the standard cropped 16:6 cover; an explicit `null` renders the neutral gradient placeholder (`from-primary-bg via-surface-elevated to-signal-bg` + an `ImageIcon`); omitting it = no cover (existing pages unchanged). The custom `hero` node stays the escape hatch and, when set, wins over `coverImage` (e.g. the event detail page's date-based fallback).
3. **The admin-settings "scope kit"** is documented as a 9-touch-point recipe (PAGE-FRAMEWORK §8.5 table): capabilities + scope variant + resolver case (`lib/core/capabilities.ts`), `get<Entity>Capabilities` (`lib/core/load-capabilities.ts`), the admin-module registry entry, the `module-map` binding, the `PATH_SCOPE_KINDS` prefix (`components/layout/settings-drawer.tsx`), the server actions (`admin-actions.ts`, each re-checking the capability), the settings module (mirror `practice-settings-module.tsx`), the capability-gated `edit-<entity>-button.tsx` in the `actions` slot, and the index page's `trail` + `heroImage`. Exemplars: `practice-settings-module.tsx` + `app/(main)/practices/admin-actions.ts`.

**Consequences.** Changed templates: `components/templates/index-template.tsx`, `components/templates/detail-template.tsx`. Migrated to the new props (banner removed): `app/(main)/circles/page.tsx`, `events/page.tsx`, `practices/page.tsx`, `journeys/page.tsx`. New entity pages compose `trail` + `heroImage` (and `coverImage` on the detail) instead of hand-rolling a header; `banner`/`hero` remain only for genuinely bespoke media. Future entities that need an in-place Settings module follow the 9-step kit rather than re-deriving it. The capability re-check inside every settings action stays the authority (the admin client bypasses RLS); the dock's role gate is UX only.

---

## ADR-412: WhatsApp chat import — export-not-scrape, dry-run first, consent-aware

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). A community admin can turn their group's exported WhatsApp history into reviewable events + housing listings. This PR ships the foundation: parser, redactor, AI classifier/extractor, and a **read-only dry-run** at `/admin/import`. No writer yet — nothing is persisted.

**Context.** An admin of a maxed-out (1025-member) WhatsApp housing group wanted its listings and local healing events on the platform without retyping. WhatsApp group history is end-to-end encrypted with **no read API** (the Cloud/Business API only sends/receives on your own number, it cannot read a group backlog). Every "scraping" route is browser-automation (whatsapp-web.js, Baileys, Selenium on WhatsApp Web) that violates WhatsApp's Terms and risks **banning the number** — an unacceptable trade for a community an admin spent years building. The user explicitly prioritized "don't fuck anything up" and offered a throwaway number for automation.

**Decision.**
1. **Export, not scrape.** Input is WhatsApp's built-in **Export chat** `.txt`. No automation touches WhatsApp's servers, so no number (main or throwaway) is ever at risk — the throwaway is moot.
2. **Reuse the events engine, don't reinvent.** Extracted events use `coerceEventExtraction` (identical shape to a poster-scanned event); the AI path mirrors `lib/ai/events-ai.ts` (one forced structured-output tool through the shared `completeRaw` chokepoint, Sonnet, voice-canon via `withVoice`, ledger-recorded, budget-gated with a new `whatsapp-import: $5/day` cap, re-checked between batches). Housing maps onto the existing `lib/listings` contract.
3. **Dry-run first (the safety guarantee).** This PR's surface is **read-only**: `previewImport` parses + classifies and returns an `ImportPreview` the operator reviews; it writes no event, listing, or storage object. Proving extraction quality precedes wiring any writer.
4. **Consent-aware by construction.** Members posted into a *private* group. Housing contacts (phones/emails) are redacted out of copy and **held back until a listing is claimed** (`lib/whatsapp/redact.ts`, digit-count guarded so prices/dates survive). The planned event writer routes through the existing **posted-event + claim-token** flow so the original poster claims and controls their event. Leans on the existing `consent_records` table + event claim handshake rather than a new consent surface.

**Why a dry-run instead of the full importer now.** The hard, verifiable parts (parse correctness, extraction quality) are deterministic-testable and write nothing; the write path mutates live `events`/`listings` and wants runtime verification. Splitting them lets the operator trust the preview before any row is created — and keeps this PR incapable of corrupting live data.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/whatsapp/{types,parse-export,redact,extract}.ts` (+ `parse-export.test.ts`, `redact.test.ts` — 32 tests), `app/(main)/admin/import/{page,import-client,actions}.tsx`, `docs/WHATSAPP-IMPORT.md`. Changed: `lib/ai/budget.ts` (`whatsapp-import` cap), `lib/admin/nav.ts` (Community → Activity → "Import from chat"). No schema change (nothing persists yet). Roadmap (writer: approve→posted-draft + approve→unclaimed-listing, plus idempotent de-dup on re-runs) tracked in `docs/WHATSAPP-IMPORT.md`.

**Addendum — image association (same PR).** Extended for media-included exports (Export chat → Attach Media / Include media → a `.zip` of `_chat.txt` + photos). The parser now captures each attachment's filename (`WhatsAppMessage.attachmentName`) from the iOS `<attached: NAME>` / Android `NAME (file attached)` markers, keeping any caption as the body. `lib/whatsapp/associate.ts` (`gatherImageNames`, pure + tested) ties a classified item to the photos posted with it (image attachments within a few messages, same author), landing on `ClassifiedItem.imageNames`. The dry-run UI takes the `_chat.txt` + image files via a multi-file picker, matches filenames to the selected images, and renders thumbnails per item — all in the browser, still no upload and no writes. New: `lib/whatsapp/associate.ts` (+ `.test.ts` — 10 more cases, 42 total). Changed: `lib/whatsapp/{types,parse-export,extract}.ts`, `app/(main)/admin/import/{actions,import-client}.tsx`. Deferred to the writer: uploading + attaching the photos, vision-reading flagged flyers via `scanEventPoster`, and `.zip` upload to skip the manual unzip.

---

## ADR-413: Posted-event sharing auto-delivers the claim link, and the publish gate matches the listing filter

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). When a member publishes an event they posted on an organizer's behalf, Frequency now auto-emails the organizer the one-time claim link (it no longer relies on the poster copy-pasting it). The public event page gained a "this is not my event / claim it" path, the publish flow blocks undated/past events, and a draft-leak in the public reads is closed.

**Context.** A member scanned several posters, published them "on behalf of" the organizers, and expected the organizers to be notified. They weren't: "share with the organizer" was a copy-to-clipboard card only (`outreach-card.tsx`) — the captured `organizer_contact` was stored but never used to send anything, and no claim email template existed. Separately, three issues compounded the confusion: (a) the organizer could never see the event in *their* drafts (drafts are owner-scoped to `posted_by_profile_id`, by design — only the claim handshake transfers it); (b) the public detail page + the `/events` discovery fallback read through the admin client and never re-applied the `status='published'` gate the poster-events migration assumes, so a draft could render on its public slug and even surface in the Catalog; and (c) a scan that misread the year wrote a past `starts_at`, which the `starts_at >= now` listing filter silently drops, so the event "vanished" with no warning.

**Decision.**
1. **Auto-deliver the claim link by email.** `publishEventDraft(..., 'posted')` now calls `deliverClaimInvite` (best-effort, never breaks publish): if `organizer_contact` is an email, it queues a new `sendEventClaimInviteEmail` (template in `lib/email.ts`, non-member transactional voice + custom footer) through the durable outbox. A raw phone/handle is **not** auto-texted (cold SMS to a non-member has no consent); those fall back to the manual copy-link card. The editor confirms "we emailed the organizer at X" via the new `claimSentTo` on `PublishResult`.
2. **Public "claim it" CTA.** `ClaimEventBanner` on the event detail page (for an unclaimed posted event) names who posted it and who the organizer is, and a button re-sends the claim link to the organizer contact **on file** — never to an address typed in the browser, and the address is never revealed — via `resendClaimInvite` + the `requestClaimLink` action (sign-in gated as light anti-abuse).
3. **Publish gate = listing filter.** `publishDraft` blocks a missing or past `starts_at` with a clear reason (the editor also warns inline and disables Publish). Publishable now implies listable.
4. **Close the draft leak.** `index-data.ts` adds `.eq('status','published')` to all three event queries, and the detail page `notFound()`s an unpublished draft for non-managers. RLS already gated this; the admin-client server reads now carry the same gate the migration assumed.

**Consequences.** New: `app/(main)/events/[slug]/claim-actions.ts`, `components/events/claim-event-banner.tsx`, the claim-invite email (`sendEventClaimInviteEmail` + builders in `lib/email.ts`). Changed: `lib/events/event-drafts.ts` (`deliverClaimInvite`, `resendClaimInvite`, `claimSentTo`), `app/(main)/events/scan/actions.ts` (date gate + thread `claimSentTo`), the draft editor + outreach card + draft page (auto-send confirmation, date warning), `index-data.ts` (status gate), the detail page (draft guard + claim banner). Also fixed a stray space in the weekly-digest greeting. SMS-to-organizer is intentionally deferred until a consented channel exists; the manual share covers texting in the meantime.

---

## ADR-414: Real-Crew creation gate (the four create capabilities) + Resonance Feed Phase 0 scaffolding

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). Authoring an event, circle, journey, or practice now requires **real Crew** (the paid tier) or a community steward; everyone else meets a one-tap "Crew is free during the beta" upgrade popup. Ships with the additive data scaffolding for the worldwide, density-adaptive Resonance Feed (see `docs/RESONANCE-FEED-ARCHITECTURE.md`). This is **Phase 0** of that plan.

**Context.** The owner's three-part vision: (1) take the gates off *running* the community so any Crew can create anything while plain Members join/adopt but never create, monetized by a free-during-beta Crew upgrade; (2) a geolocation-aware "resonance" feed that always has content (ripple outward when the local area is sparse); (3) a founder prompt + location-privacy story. Two facts shaped the gate. First, creation gating was **scattered and inconsistent**: events read the real `membership_tier`, practices keyed off `BETA_OPEN_ACCESS || atLeastRole('crew')` and showed the create button to everyone, and circles + journeys were ungated beyond sign-in. Second, `BETA_OPEN_ACCESS` (ADR for the beta switch) overrides every signed-in member's *effective* tier to Crew, so a gate that reads the effective tier can never fire the upgrade popup. The owner chose "**real Crew to create, free one-tap**" (AskUserQuestion), across **all four** entities.

**Decision.**
1. **One resolver, four capabilities.** `lib/core/capabilities.ts` gains `event.create | circle.create | journey.create | practice.create`, granted in the `global` scope when `isPaid(realTier) || atLeastRole(role,'crew') || isStaff`. The single pure resolver is the definition; the scattered inline checks collapse into it (ADR-017).
2. **Read the REAL tier, not the beta override.** A new `Viewer.realTier` carries the true DB `membership_tier` *before* the `BETA_OPEN_ACCESS` override. `lib/auth.ts` exposes `realMembershipTier` (the raw column) alongside the overridden `membershipTier`; `lib/core/load-capabilities.ts` threads it into the viewer. So the upgrade popup fires for a genuinely free member **even while the beta auto-grants Crew everywhere else** — exactly the "real Crew to create" intent. `capabilityGaps` rung 2 bumps `realTier` too, so a free member's missing create caps attribute to `needs-paid-tier` (the rung the popup sells).
3. **UX is the popup, server is the law.** Each create entry point renders `CrewGateButton` (with per-reason `UPGRADE_COPY`: `create-event/circle/journey/practice`) so a non-Crew member sees the builder link replaced by the free-beta upgrade lightbox. Every create **page** (`/{events,circles,journeys,practices}/new`) and every create **action** re-checks via `assertCanCreate` / `canCreate('*.create')` (new helpers in `load-capabilities.ts`). The button is convenience; the server re-check is the authority (the admin client bypasses RLS).
4. **Practices realigned.** Its create button used to show for everyone during the beta; it now shows for every signed-in member but routes non-Crew to the popup, and both `authorizeCreatePractice` copies (`create-actions.ts`, `actions.ts`) drop the `BETA_OPEN_ACCESS` axis for `canCreate('practice.create')`. The **PENDING-for-Host-review** behavior is unchanged: a non-host author still creates unpublished.
5. **Events left on its equivalent inline check.** `index-data.ts` already reads the real `membership_tier` (via the admin client, which the beta never overrides) plus steward roles, so it is functionally identical to the capability. Consolidating it onto the capability is a follow-up, noted not done, to keep this PR's blast radius bounded.
6. **Phase 0 schema scaffolding (additive, opt-in, nothing reads it yet).** Migration `20260821000000_resonance_phase0_scaffolding.sql` adds three tables: `suggestion_hidden` (the member-controlled "X / hide this person", owner-RLS), `resonance_density_cells` (per-fuzzed-geocell activity rollup for the adaptive radius, service-role only), and `member_match_prefs` (one opt-in row **reserving** the future romance + astrology baselines: `connect_intent`, `romance_mode`, `astrology_opt_in`, `birth_data`, all off/null, owner-RLS). Romance mode and astrology matching are **deliberately not built** (owner decision); the row exists so they have a governed home the day they ship. No swipe mechanics; streaks-as-a-quiet-signal and the radius slider live in the strategy doc's later phases.

**Why real-tier over a dedicated flag.** Reusing the existing entitlement axis (with a real-vs-effective split) means flipping `BETA_OPEN_ACCESS` off later changes nothing about creation: the gate already reads the real tier. The one-tap free upgrade (`toggleMembership`, `/upgrade`) flips the real `membership_tier` to Crew, so the popup is a working funnel today, not a placeholder.

**Consequences.** Changed (gate): `lib/core/capabilities.ts` (+test), `lib/auth.ts`, `lib/core/load-capabilities.ts`, `components/crew/upgrade-lightbox.tsx`, `components/compose/new-circle-compose.tsx`, `components/studio/journey/new-journey-button.tsx`, `components/studio/practice/new-practice-button.tsx`, `lib/circles/index-data.ts`, `components/page-editor/blocks/circles.tsx`, and the circles/journeys/practices index + `/new` pages + create actions + the channel page. New: the scaffolding migration + `docs/RESONANCE-FEED-ARCHITECTURE.md` + the BUILD-LIST phase timeline. Verified on prod (`azsqfeonabsbmemvddqd`): three tables created, RLS enabled, member tables 3 policies each, density table service-role-only (0 policies, intentional `rls_enabled_no_policy` INFO), no new ERROR advisors. The three tables are reached untyped until `lib/database.types.ts` regenerates (ADR-246) — deferred because no code reads them yet. **Reversible:** `BETA_OPEN_ACCESS` and the real-tier split are independent; dropping the three tables removes the scaffolding with no impact on the gate.

---

## ADR-415: Resonance Feed Phase 1 — the blended "For you" rank + people in the feed

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). The feed's "For you" lens (`sort='relevant'`) is now a blended resonance rank instead of a plain engagement sort, and a "people you'd click with" row rides inside it. Phase 1 of the plan (`docs/RESONANCE-FEED-ARCHITECTURE.md`, ADR-414). No schema change.

**Context.** Phase 0 shipped the access gate + the scaffolding tables. The composition layer was still missing: the feed ranked posts by `engagement_score` then recency (`lib/feed-rank.ts`), ignored the resonance graph and proximity entirely, and surfaced people only in the separate Community directory. The shipped graph (orbit + resonance edges), embeddings, and proximity RPCs were unused by the feed.

**Decision.**
1. **A pure, five-signal blend** (`lib/feed/blend-rank.ts`, unit-tested): `blendScore` weights proximity + graph + engagement + recency (interest folds into graph until post-content embeddings land), then `blendRank` sorts and applies a `diversityRerank` that caps any one author in the primary band. FAIL-SAFE: each signal is optional and its weight redistributes when absent, so a post is never penalized for missing a signal, and with no resonance + no geo the blend reduces to today's recency-led behavior.
2. **The graph signal is the viewer's resonance map** (`lib/feed/viewer-resonance.ts`): `authorId → strength [0,1]`, the MAX of the saturated `my_orbit` co-presence and the reciprocal `resonance_edges` score. Request-cached, fail-safe (empty when resonance is off). Proximity reads the `distance_m` the existing `feed_for_viewer` RPC already computes — Phase 1 just passes the viewer's coords in the 'relevant' lens too, which only POPULATES distance (the RPC still orders by distance for 'nearby' alone, so selection is unchanged).
3. **People enter the feed** (`components/feed/feed-people-strip.tsx` + `lib/feed/feed-people.ts`): the existing real-signal suggestion engine (`getPeopleSuggestions`: shared circles + mutual connections), now also filtered by the Phase 0 `suggestion_hidden` list, rendered with the shared `PersonCard` after the first few posts, streamed behind `<Suspense>`. Renders nothing when there's no genuine suggestion.
4. **Only the "For you" lens changes.** 'recent', 'nearby', and 'story' stay literal (recency / nearest / chronological).

**Consequences.** New: `lib/feed/{blend-rank.ts,blend-rank.test.ts,viewer-resonance.ts,feed-people.ts}`, `components/feed/feed-people-strip.tsx`. Changed: `components/feed/feed-list.tsx` (blend branch + people strip + geo-arg for 'relevant'). `suggestion_hidden` is read via the untyped cast (ADR-246). The hide WRITE (the X button) and the radius slider are Phase 3; post-content embeddings deepen the interest signal in a later phase. Gate: tsc + eslint + authz + 2368 tests (11 new). Reversible: the 'relevant' branch falls back to `rankFeedPosts` by deleting the blend call; no data migration to undo.

---

## ADR-416: Resonance Feed Phase 2 — density rollup, adaptive radius, founder prompt

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). The feed now reads how alive a member's corner is and adapts: it widens the radius when the area is sparse (the "ripple"), and shows a founder prompt when nobody is around yet (or a location nudge when the member has no location). Phase 2 of the plan (`docs/RESONANCE-FEED-ARCHITECTURE.md` §4–§5, ADR-414/415).

**Context.** Phase 0 added the empty `resonance_density_cells` table; Phase 1 added the blend. Nothing filled the table or read it, so the feed had no sense of local density: a member in a quiet corner saw an empty 'nearby' lens with no path forward, and a member with no location got no nudge. The "ripple" (always-full feed) and the founder prompt both needed a per-cell activity signal.

**Decision.**
1. **The rollup is a SQL function** (`refresh_resonance_density_cells()`, migration `20260822000000`): a full nightly rebuild of `resonance_density_cells` aggregating, PER FUZZED GEOCELL, discoverable members + recent events + live circles + recent posts into counts and a 0..1 `density_score` (circles weigh most, then events, members, chatter). SECURITY DEFINER, service-role only. Keyed to the fuzzed ~1.1km cell, counts only, no identities, no raw coordinates. Wired into the existing nightly `refresh-traits` cron as a best-effort, fail-safe step (`lib/resonance/density.ts`, authz-delegated like the sibling edge/embedding refreshers). Verified on prod: 4 cells written.
2. **The ripple + state are pure math** (`lib/feed/ripple.ts`, unit-tested). `localActivityState(hasLocation, density)` → `no-location | founder | active`: an empty, anchor-less, near-empty cell is 'founder'; a standing circle / upcoming event / enough people keeps it 'active'. `rippleRadiusM(base, density)` widens the radius linearly as the cell empties (x1 when alive, up to x8 when empty / unknown), never narrowing below the member's base — so a sparse corner still sees something.
3. **The server seam** (`lib/feed/density.ts`, `getLocalActivity`) reads the member's fuzzed geocell + radius + the density cell and returns the state + effective radius. Request-cached, fail-safe (any error → no-location at base radius, feed still renders).
4. **The surfaces** (`components/feed/local-corner-card.tsx`): a `<LocalCornerCard>` on the home feed (not Story), streamed behind `<Suspense>`, that renders the location nudge (with the never-share-your-exact-spot promise → `/settings/profile`), the founder prompt (start a circle via the gated `NewCircleCompose` / host an event / invite people), or nothing when the corner is already alive. The 'nearby' lens now uses the rippled `effectiveRadiusM`.

**Consequences.** New: migration `20260822000000_resonance_density_rollup.sql`, `lib/feed/{ripple.ts,ripple.test.ts,density.ts}`, `lib/resonance/density.ts`, `components/feed/local-corner-card.tsx`. Changed: `app/api/cron/refresh-traits/route.ts` (density step), `app/(main)/feed/page.tsx` (card + rippled nearby radius). The `nexus_region_id` mapping (point-in-region) is deferred — the ripple uses geocell distance for now; the column stays nullable. Gate: tsc, eslint, authz, 2380 tests (12 new in `ripple.test.ts`). Reversible: drop the function + delete the card mount; no destructive data change.

---

## ADR-417: Resonance Feed Phase 3 — feed-reach slider, hide control, streak signal

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). Members now control how far their feed reaches with a slider, remove a suggested person with an "X" (so we never suggest them again), and a quiet shared-streak signal nudges people-suggestions. Phase 3 of the plan (`docs/RESONANCE-FEED-ARCHITECTURE.md`, ADR-414/415/416). No schema change.

**Context.** Phase 1 added the people strip + the `suggestion_hidden` READ filter; Phase 2 added the adaptive radius. Two member controls were still missing: a way to set the base feed radius (the dating-app-style reach slider the owner asked for), and the WRITE side of the hide control (the "X"). The owner also wanted streaks to act as a quiet match signal.

**Decision.**
1. **Feed-reach slider** (`components/settings/feed-radius-slider.tsx` on `/settings/connections`): a self-contained client slider over `feed_radius_m` (5km to 500km), committing on release through the existing self-authorized `saveMyConnectionPrefs` (extended with a clamped `feedRadiusM`). Distinct from the discoverability radius (who can find ME); this is how far MY feed casts. The copy is explicit that the ripple still widens it when the area is quiet, so the slider is a floor + preference, not a cage. `getMyConnectionPrefs` now returns `feedRadiusM`.
2. **Hide control (the write)** (`app/(main)/feed/people-actions.ts` + `components/feed/dismissable-suggestion.tsx`): an "X" on each suggested person, optimistic (the card disappears instantly), backed by `hideSuggestionAction` which upserts the caller's `suggestion_hidden` row. Self-authorized (profile_id is always the caller), so it can never touch anyone else's list. No swipe mechanics, just a calm dismiss. The Phase 1 read filter already keeps hidden people out, so they never resurface.
3. **Streak as a quiet signal** (`lib/feed/feed-people.ts`): the feed people suggestions read the viewer's + candidates' `current_streak` in one query and mark `bothStreaking` when both keep a streak of >= 3 days, which edges those matches up and adds a soft "you both keep a streak" reason. Never prominent, never a leaderboard, exactly as scoped.

**Consequences.** New: `components/settings/feed-radius-slider.tsx`, `app/(main)/feed/people-actions.ts`, `components/feed/dismissable-suggestion.tsx`. Changed: `lib/connections/connection-settings.ts` (+`feedRadiusM`), `lib/connections/connection-settings-actions.ts` (clamped feed-radius write + `/feed` revalidate), `app/(main)/settings/connections/page.tsx` (mount), `lib/feed/feed-people.ts` (streak signal), `components/feed/feed-people-strip.tsx` (dismiss wrapper + streak reason). `feed_radius_m` / `suggestion_hidden` reached untyped (ADR-088/246). Gate: tsc, eslint, authz, 2380 tests. Reversible: remove the slider mount + the dismiss wrapper; no data migration to undo.

---

## ADR-418: Resonance Feed Phase 4 — meet-safely guidance + member verification scaffold

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). Members get clear meet-safely guidance where the feed introduces them to people, the verification badge surface is in place, and the verification flow itself is a deliberate, documented deferral. Phase 4 (the last build phase) of the plan (`docs/RESONANCE-FEED-ARCHITECTURE.md` §6, ADR-414→417).

**Context.** The owner's safety idea: people meet at a real circle or public event (witnessed, public, low-risk), and we make staying safe easy; verification is wanted but the METHOD (phone / ID / vouch) is a genuine product decision, not something to invent unilaterally. So Phase 4 splits cleanly: ship the safety guidance + the verification INFRASTRUCTURE now, defer the verification FLOW.

**Decision.**
1. **Verification scaffold** (migration `20260823000000`): additive `profiles.verified_at`, `verification_method` (RESERVED: phone/id/vouch/email, null today), and `meetup_safety_ack_at`. Nothing sets `verified_at` yet, so no badge appears until a flow ships, exactly like the romance/astrology scaffold (ADR-414). Applied + verified on prod.
2. **The verified badge** (`components/ui/verified-badge.tsx`): a calm check by a name, rendered ONLY when `verified_at` is set. Reusable; first surfaced on the feed people strip (the meetup-relevant surface). The feed people read now carries `verified` per person.
3. **Meet-safely note** (`components/safety/meetup-safety-note.tsx`): a quiet line under the "people you'd click with" row — meet at a circle or public event first, here's how — that shows once and then stays quiet (a "Got it" writes `meetup_safety_ack_at` via the self-authorized `acknowledgeMeetupSafetyAction`). Links to the new guide.
4. **The guide** (`content/help/safety/meeting-people-safely.md`): meet at a circle/event, keep early meetings public, tell someone, trust your gut, the never-share-your-exact-location promise, and how to remove / block / report. Ties into the existing reporting + blocking, not a rebuild.

**Why defer the verification flow.** A real identity check is a product + trust + cost decision (which method, what it gates, how it's stored). Scaffolding the columns + the badge means the day that decision is made, the surfaces already exist; until then the safety guidance (meet in the open, around something real) is what keeps meetings safe, and the doc says so plainly.

**Consequences.** New: migration `20260823000000_member_verification_scaffold.sql`, `components/ui/verified-badge.tsx`, `components/safety/meetup-safety-note.tsx`, `content/help/safety/meeting-people-safely.md`. Changed: `app/(main)/feed/people-actions.ts` (ack action), `lib/feed/feed-people.ts` (verified + ack reads), `components/feed/feed-people-strip.tsx` (badge + note). The three new columns are reached untyped (ADR-246). Gate: tsc, eslint, authz, 2380 tests. With Phase 4 merged, the resonance-feed build is complete through Phase 4; Phase 5 (romance + astrology) stays deliberately deferred on its Phase 0 scaffolding.

---

## ADR-419: Resonance Feed Phase 5 — romance mode + astrology matching (activated)

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). The Phase 0 `member_match_prefs` scaffolding is now ACTIVATED: members can opt into an astrology compatibility signal and into romance matching, both off by default. Phase 5 of the plan (`docs/RESONANCE-FEED-ARCHITECTURE.md` §6). No schema change (the columns were reserved in ADR-414).

**Context.** Phases 0 to 4 deliberately deferred romance + astrology, reserving `member_match_prefs.{connect_intent, romance_mode, astrology_opt_in, birth_data}`. The owner then asked to "run phase 5". Both features touch safety + sensitive data, so the build is conservative: opt-in everything, mutual-only romance, no swipe, and the meet-safely guidance reinforced throughout.

**Decision.**
1. **Astrology engine** (`lib/astrology/signs.ts`, pure + unit-tested): sun-sign from a birth DATE (no ephemeris; time/place reserved on `birth_data` for a future fuller chart), element/modality, and a symmetric element-based `signCompatibility` in [0,1] with a plain, never-overstated reason. 6 tests cover cusp boundaries + symmetry.
2. **Match prefs** (`lib/match/prefs.ts` + `app/(main)/settings/connections/match-actions.ts`): read/write `member_match_prefs` through self-authorized upsert (profile_id = caller). `connect_intent` constrained to a known set; `birth_data` validated to `YYYY-MM-DD`. A `MatchPrefsForm` on `/settings/connections` exposes both toggles + the birth date (only the sign is used, the date is never shown).
3. **Astrology signal in the platonic feed** (`lib/feed/feed-people.ts`): a quiet "your signs click" reason, ONLY when the viewer AND the candidate both opted in and both have a birth date. Off entirely otherwise; lightly sorts suggestions, never overturns the graph spine.
4. **Romance lane** (`lib/match/romance.ts` + `components/feed/romance-strip.tsx`): STRICTLY opt-in and MUTUAL. Renders an "Open to connection" strip on the feed ONLY when the viewer has romance mode on, showing only OTHER members who also opted in, drawn from the same real-signal pool (shared circles + mutual connections) the platonic strip uses, ranked by astrology when both opted in. Honors the hide list, shows the verified badge + the meet-safely note. No swipe mechanics. Invisible to everyone who hasn't opted in.

**Safety posture.** Everything is off by default; romance is never one-sided (both parties must opt in to appear to each other); candidates are people the viewer already shares community context with, not strangers from nowhere; exact location is never involved; and the meet-safely guidance (ADR-418) rides along. The existing double opt-in to actually connect (resonance_matches) and report/block remain the backstop.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/astrology/{signs.ts,signs.test.ts}`, `lib/match/{prefs.ts,romance.ts}`, `app/(main)/settings/connections/match-actions.ts`, `components/settings/match-prefs-form.tsx`, `components/feed/romance-strip.tsx`, `content/help/connecting/matching-and-romance.md`. Changed: `app/(main)/settings/connections/page.tsx` (mount), `lib/feed/feed-people.ts` (astro signal), `components/feed/feed-people-strip.tsx` (astro reason), `app/(main)/feed/page.tsx` (romance strip). `member_match_prefs` reached untyped (ADR-246). Gate: tsc, eslint, authz, 2386 tests (6 new). The resonance-feed build is now complete through Phase 5. Reversible: the strips return null when nobody opts in; removing the mounts + the prefs form fully retracts it with no data migration.

---

## ADR-420: Verification flow — "showed up" (attendance-based), gating the romance lane

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-26). The verification flow deferred in ADR-418 is now built: a member becomes verified the first time they physically check in at a real event, and an unverified member does not appear in the romance lane. Owner decisions (AskUserQuestion): method = **"showed up" (attendance) only**; **verified-to-appear** in the romance lane.

**Context.** ADR-418 scaffolded `profiles.verified_at` + `verification_method` + the badge but deferred the FLOW (phone / ID / vouch) as a product + cost decision. The owner chose the attendance baseline: it is the truest real-person signal for an in-person community, costs nothing, needs no new provider, and reinforces the core loop (go to a circle/event). The platform already records a real door check-in (`checkInEvent` -> an idempotent `event_checkin` engagement event, gated to a 'going' RSVP at a started event), so the signal already exists.

**Decision.**
1. **Verify on check-in** (`lib/verification/attendance.ts`): `checkInEvent` calls `markVerifiedByAttendance(profileId)` on every valid check-in. It stamps `verified_at = now()` + `verification_method = 'attendance'` ONCE (only when `verified_at` is null), so a stronger method added later is never clobbered, and re-runs are free. Self-scoped (checkInEvent resolved the caller and passes only that id) + fail-safe (never breaks a check-in); authz-delegated.
2. **Backfill** (migration `20260824000000`): any member who already attended (an `event_checkin` engagement event) gets `verified_at` stamped to their earliest check-in, method 'attendance'. Idempotent, non-destructive (only fills nulls). Applied on prod (a no-op today: 0 check-ins in the new beta, correct for the future).
3. **Verified-to-appear in romance** (`lib/match/romance.ts`): the romance lane now only ever shows VERIFIED mutual opt-ins, and an unverified member never appears to anyone. An unverified-but-opted-in member can still browse, and the strip nudges them: "show up to an event and check in to verify." The platonic feed + the badge are unchanged (the badge simply renders now, once real check-ins happen).

**Why attendance over phone/ID.** Phone needs an SMS provider + recurring cost for a medium signal; ID is high friction + per-check cost + PII storage. Attendance is free, distinctive, and makes the badge MEAN "this person actually turns up", which is exactly the safety signal that matters before an in-person meetup. Phone remains an easy future add (a settings "verify with your phone" button writing method 'phone') if a pre-first-event badge is wanted.

**Consequences.** New: migration `20260824000000_verify_by_attendance.sql`, `lib/verification/attendance.ts`. Changed: `app/(main)/events/actions.ts` (checkInEvent verifies), `lib/match/romance.ts` (+`viewerVerified`, verified-only candidates), `components/feed/romance-strip.tsx` (verify nudge). Help: `meeting-people-safely.md` + `matching-and-romance.md` + `events.md` updated to say checking in verifies you. `verified_at` reached untyped (ADR-246). Gate: tsc, eslint, authz, 2386 tests. Reversible: the runtime call + the romance filter can be removed; the backfill is data-only (clearing it would un-verify real attendees).

---

## ADR-421: Onboarding avatars are downscaled on pick (fix silent localStorage loss) + add-photo nudge

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-27). New-member avatars are center-cropped + JPEG-compressed to ~512px the moment they're picked, fixing a silent failure where a chosen profile photo never saved; plus a dismissible feed nudge for anyone who still lands without one.

**Context.** A new member (@unity) reported their profile pic "didn't come through". Diagnosis on prod: the profile existed with all text answers intact but `avatar_url` null and nothing in the `avatars` bucket. Root cause: the signed-out beta induction parks the chosen avatar as a base64 data URL in **localStorage** to survive the magic-link sign-in hop (`app/onboarding/beta/induction.tsx` `persistForAuth` → `/complete` finalizer uploads it). A raw phone photo is multiple MB; its base64 overflows localStorage's ~5MB quota, so `setItem` throws `QuotaExceededError`, which a `catch` swallows. The member SAW their photo (preview via `URL.createObjectURL`, size-independent), so it looked saved, then vanished. This hits a large share of mobile signups using a real photo. The `avatars` bucket + its owner-scoped INSERT policy are correct; the failure was entirely client-side.

**Decision.** Downscale on pick: `lib/images/downscale-image.ts` `downscaleImageFile` center-crops to a square and re-encodes as a ~512px JPEG (tens of KB), called in `handleFileChange`. Every downstream path then uses the small image: the localStorage park fits with room to spare, and the immediate upload is faster. Best-effort: any failure (odd format, no canvas, decode error) returns the original file, so picking a photo never throws. PLUS a safety net (`components/feed/avatar-nudge.tsx`): a dismissible "add a photo" feed card for any signed-in member with no avatar, catching every other failure mode (cross-browser magic-link, transient upload error); it links to Settings → Profile and disappears once a photo is added.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/images/downscale-image.ts`, `components/feed/avatar-nudge.tsx`. Changed: `app/onboarding/beta/induction.tsx` (`handleFileChange` async + compress), `app/(main)/feed/page.tsx` (read avatar_url + render the nudge). The cross-browser magic-link hop is now covered by the nudge; a server-side avatar stash remains a possible durable upgrade. @unity's photo was never uploaded, so it can't be recovered; they can add one from Settings → Profile. Gate: tsc, eslint, 2386 tests.

---

## ADR-422: Profile-image uploads run server-side (fix avatars failing with "row-level security policy")

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-27). Avatar and header uploads now go through a server action that writes with the service role after resolving the caller's auth user id from the session, instead of the browser Supabase client writing to storage directly. Fixes a hard upload failure and closes the remaining gap from ADR-421's "server-side avatar stash" note.

**Context.** The same member from ADR-421 (@unity) still couldn't set a photo, now with a visible error: **"Upload failed: new row violates row-level security policy."** Diagnosis on prod (`azsqfeonabsbmemvddqd`): the `avatars` owner-INSERT policy is correct and, simulated under @unity's authenticated JWT, evaluates `true` for their path `{auth_uid}/avatar.jpg`. Storage logs showed the real request: `POST 400 /object/avatars/8b0d1087…/avatar.jpg`. The only way that path 400s while the policy passes for an authenticated caller is that the request arrived **without a session** — it ran as `anon`, and the policy is `TO authenticated`, so it fell to default-deny. Root cause: both the settings form (`profile-form.tsx`) and the onboarding finalizer (`complete/finalizer.tsx`) uploaded via the **browser** client (`@/lib/supabase/client`). Under SSR-cookie auth the access token lives in cookies the *server* reads; the browser client frequently has no session (notably right after the magic-link hop, and on mobile Safari), so `storage.upload()` goes out unauthenticated. The page still renders because the server read the cookie — masking the problem. A profile's `id` need not equal its `auth_user_id` here, but both the upload path and the policy key off the auth user id consistently, so that decoupling was not the cause.

**Decision.** Move every profile-image write to the server. New `lib/storage/profile-images.ts` `uploadProfileImage(authUserId, bytes, contentType, kind)` validates size (≤5MB) + mime and uploads via the **service-role** admin client to the unchanged path `{authUserId}/{kind}.{ext}`, returning the cache-busted public URL. Two thin server actions resolve + verify the caller first: `uploadProfileImageAction` (settings, takes `FormData`) and `uploadPendingAvatar` (onboarding, decodes the parked data URL). The settings form resizes to JPEG client-side then posts the blob; the finalizer hands the parked data URL to the action. The service-role write is self-scoped — the path is always the *resolved* caller's auth id, never client-supplied — so it passes the authz-contract guard without weakening ownership.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/storage/profile-images.ts`. Changed: `app/(main)/settings/profile/actions.ts` (+`uploadProfileImageAction`), `app/(main)/settings/profile/profile-form.tsx` (server action instead of browser storage; drops the `@/lib/supabase/client` import), `app/onboarding/beta/actions.ts` (+`uploadPendingAvatar`), `app/onboarding/beta/complete/finalizer.tsx` (server upload; drops the client decode + upload). The owner-scoped INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE policies and public read-by-URL are untouched — the write path simply no longer depends on a browser session. Gate: tsc, eslint, authz-contract. Reversible: the actions can fall back to client uploads, but that reintroduces the anon-write failure.

---

## ADR-423: Spotlight — opt-in profile mini-site, the access spine (capability + per-user flag, off by default)

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-27). The first round of the configurable-profile vision ships as **the access spine only**: a member's opt-in public mini-site (a linktree/personal page) named **Spotlight**, gated by one capability seam and a per-user flag that is OFF for everyone until an admin turns it on. No public page or theming yet — those are the next rounds, built on this spine.

**Context.** The owner wants editable, gamified profiles + a public linktree/mini-site page "for all the profiles", explicitly: build it but keep it **off for everyone by default, with a way to turn it on per user to mess with**, and the public page available to **Crew+** (Spaces will get richer immersive variants later). A multi-agent design + adversarial review pass produced the framework and caught two naming landmines and several privacy/RLS requirements. **Naming:** the obvious "Studio" is **locked** (the creation tool, and reserved for the future Calm/Studio *mode* axis) and "Signal" is a **retired rank** (zero-hits canon) — so the feature is named **Spotlight** (docs/NAMING.md, this ADR).

**Decision.**
1. **One capability seam, profile-scoped.** `lib/core/capabilities.ts` gains `spotlight.manage` and `spotlight.view`, resolved in the existing `profile` scope. `spotlight.manage` → the owner of an *enabled* Spotlight (or a janitor, for moderation). `spotlight.view` → a **Crew+** viewer, read from the **real** tier (`realTier ?? tier`, ADR-414) so the beta open-access grant can't widen who reaches the public page. The `profile` scope carries the owner's flags (`ownerSpotlightEnabled`/`ownerSpotlightPublished`); `getProfileCapabilities` reads them from the owner's `meta`. No new `Viewer` field — opt-in is OWNER state, not viewer-global.
2. **Two independent flags in `profiles.meta`, both default-false.** `meta.spotlight.enabled` (set up / "turned on to mess with") and `meta.spotlight.published` (live at the public URL) — separate so "on" never implies "public". `lib/profile/spotlight-flags.ts` is the single typed source of truth (readers + pure merge helpers + unit tests); no flag path is ever a loose string literal twice. No migration (meta is free-form jsonb).
3. **The per-user switch is a janitor admin action.** `app/(main)/admin/members/spotlight-actions.ts` `toggleSpotlightEnabled` (janitor-guarded, mirrors `economy-actions.ts`, audited as `spotlight.toggle`) flips only `enabled`, never `published`. Surfaced as a "Turn on Spotlight" button per member in the admin roster. The member list reads `meta` server-side to derive a single boolean and **never sends the raw meta blob to the client** (it holds acquisition/UTM/streak PII).

**Why a spine first.** Each later round (the public `/spotlight/[handle]` page with its strict column allowlist; the theme picker wiring the dormant `profile_theme` column; earned cosmetics; the Ember streak companion; Spaces' immersive variants) plugs into this exact seam — one capability check, one `meta.spotlight.*` namespace, one theme value. Shipping the gate first means everything after is additive and stays off-by-default by construction.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/profile/spotlight-flags.ts` (+ test, 5 cases), `app/(main)/admin/members/spotlight-actions.ts`. Changed: `lib/core/capabilities.ts` (+2 caps, profile-scope flags, +2 resolver tests — 41 pass), `lib/core/load-capabilities.ts` (`getProfileCapabilities` reads owner meta), `app/(main)/admin/members/{member-admin.tsx,page.tsx}` (toggle UI + derived boolean, meta stripped), `docs/NAMING.md` (Spotlight locked). Privacy guardrails carried into the next rounds (from the review): the public page must use an explicit column allowlist via the admin client (anon visitors get zero RLS), never select `meta`/contact/geo, and publishing stays Crew+ and explicit until a real minor-age signal exists. Gate: tsc, eslint, authz-contract, vitest. Reversible: the caps resolve to nothing while every `meta.spotlight.enabled` is false (the default), so the spine is dormant until deliberately switched on.

---

## ADR-424: Spotlight round 2 — the public page is public-when-published (a shareable mini-site)

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-27). The Spotlight access spine (ADR-423) gains its visible surface: a public `/spotlight/[handle]` page and an owner publish control. This ADR also **resolves the view-access question** ADR-423 left cautious: a *published* Spotlight is viewable by anyone with the link, including signed-out non-members.

**Context.** A Spotlight is a linktree/personal mini-site whose entire purpose is sharing — to friends, to people who aren't on Frequency yet. ADR-423 reserved a Crew+ `spotlight.view` capability as a careful default, but a page only members can see can't be shared the way a linktree is. The product question had to be answered to build the page. The owner's intent ("web pages for all the profiles... act as a linktree or mini site... share info and display stats") is unambiguously public-facing.

**Decision.**
1. **Public-when-published.** `app/spotlight/[handle]/page.tsx` is a TOP-LEVEL route (outside the auth-gated `(main)` group, which redirects signed-out users), so a shared link opens for anyone. It is per-request and **fail-closed**: not-found / inactive / system / not-published all 404, with no "not public yet" copy to police. The Crew+ `spotlight.view` capability from ADR-423 is therefore **not** the public-page gate; it stays reserved for future in-app discovery affordances (it grants nothing today). Safety shifts entirely to the publish side — see below.
2. **Two locks before anything is public, both default-off.** A page is public only when BOTH an admin has turned the member's Spotlight **on** (`meta.spotlight.enabled`, ADR-423) AND the owner has explicitly **published** it (`meta.spotlight.published`). The owner publish control (`setSpotlightPublished`, self-scoped, requires enabled-first) lives in Settings → Profile and only appears once enabled. So nothing is public by accident, and the platform retains the master switch.
3. **A strict column allowlist is the privacy boundary.** Anonymous visitors get zero RLS on `profiles`, so `lib/spotlight/privacy.ts` defines `SPOTLIGHT_SELECT` — the literal, explicit `.select()` argument (never `*`, never a row spread). A unit test asserts it contains no contact/geo/auth/billing column and never the `meta` blob. The publish flag is read in a SEPARATE gate query (from `meta`) and never returned. The page shows name, avatar, header, bio, website, role/tier badge, region label, and the streak/gems stats the member chose to display — plus their upcoming hosted events.
4. **Themed by the profile.** The page wraps its subtree in `[data-skin]` from `resolveSkin(profile_theme)`, so the built-in skin CSS cascades without touching the app shell. The theme PICKER (letting owners choose) is round 3.

**Why two flags, not one.** "Turned on to mess with" (enabled) and "live on the internet" (published) are different risk levels. Splitting them lets the platform enable a member for setup without anything going public until that member deliberately publishes.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/spotlight/{privacy.ts,privacy.test.ts,data.ts}`, `components/spotlight/spotlight-page.tsx`, `app/spotlight/[handle]/page.tsx`. Changed: `app/(main)/settings/profile/{actions.ts (setSpotlightPublished, self-scoped),page.tsx,profile-form.tsx (publish control + view link)}`. The in-app profile-page CTA + the theme picker are round 3 (which also edits `people/[handle]`). Minor-age signal remains deferred (ADR-423/418): the page stays opt-in + admin-gated + owner-published, with no contact/location ever shown, until a real age flag exists. Gate: tsc, eslint, authz-contract, vitest. Reversible: the route 404s for everyone while no one is both enabled and published (the default); deleting the route + the publish control fully retracts it with no data migration.

---

## ADR-425: Spotlight round 3 — constrained theme picker (wire profile_theme) + in-app link

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-27). The owner can now choose a look for their Spotlight + profile from a governed set of skins, wiring the previously-dormant `profiles.profile_theme` column. Round 3 of Spotlight (ADR-423/424). No migration.

**Context.** `profile_theme` has existed unused; rounds 1–2 read it but nothing let a member set it. The owner wants expressive-but-safe profiles. The "constrained but expressive" research lesson (Linear's 3-variable themes, design tokens, SpaceHey blocking raw CSS/JS) says: let people pick from a governed kit, never inject markup or raw colors.

**Decision.**
1. **A curated allowlist + one choke point.** `lib/theme/profile-skins.ts` exposes `PROFILE_SKINS` (a subset of the app `SKINS`, each carrying a reserved `requiredItem?` for the future cosmetics gate) and `resolveProfileSkin()` — the SINGLE owner-aware function every Spotlight render site calls (replacing the inline `resolveSkin`). So earned/cosmetic skins later are an additive change in one place, not a render-site sweep.
2. **Owner-only, validated write.** `updateProfileTheme(themeId)` (self-scoped to the caller's `auth_user_id`, requires Spotlight enabled) validates against the allowlist before writing `profile_theme` — no raw color/CSS value can ever reach the column.
3. **A visible picker.** `components/spotlight/profile-theme-picker.tsx` previews each skin by applying its own `[data-skin]` to the swatch, surfaced in the Settings → Profile Spotlight section (only once enabled). Optimistic with rollback on error.
4. **In-app discovery.** The profile page (`people/[handle]`) shows a "Spotlight" link to anyone when the member has a published page (derived from meta server-side; the blob never reaches the client).

**Consequences.** New: `lib/theme/profile-skins.ts`, `lib/profile/profile-theme-actions.ts`, `components/spotlight/profile-theme-picker.tsx`. Changed: `components/spotlight/spotlight-page.tsx` (resolveProfileSkin choke point), `app/(main)/settings/profile/{page.tsx,profile-form.tsx}` (picker + profile_theme prop), `app/(main)/people/[handle]/page.tsx` (Spotlight link). Today the picker offers the two built-in skins (default, midnight); the cosmetics layer adds earned skins by extending `PROFILE_SKINS` + gating on `requiredItem`, with no render changes. Gate: tsc, eslint, authz-contract. Reversible: removing the picker leaves `profile_theme` dormant again with no data change.

---

## ADR-426: Janitor "Act as member" (identity impersonation) + role-specific profile edit

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-27). A janitor can now BECOME a member ("Act as") — a real Supabase session swap so every read AND write runs as that member — with a persistent banner, one-tap Exit, and an audit trail. The profile Edit affordances are now role-specific: admins get basic edit + the account manager; janitors additionally get Act-as.

**Context.** The owner wanted, on a member's profile, a janitor "view as member with full admin control… as if it was their account," with the Edit button differing by role: admin = basic profile control + settings, janitor = full control. The existing `view-as` (lib/view-as.ts) is a role/Space PREVIEW (downgrade-only) — it never assumes a specific member's identity, so it can't satisfy "act as @unity." The app keys identity on the Supabase session (`auth.uid()`), and many writes use `getMyProfileId()` / `.eq('auth_user_id', user.id)` / the session RLS client — so the only way EVERY path acts as the member is to swap the actual session, not overlay an app-level effective id (owner decision, AskUserQuestion: "True Act-as" over a read-only or write-blocked overlay).

**Decision.**
1. **Session swap, gated on the REAL janitor.** `actAsMember(targetProfileId)` (`app/(main)/impersonate-actions.ts`) re-reads the caller's web_role from the DB (never the preview-effective role) and requires `janitor` BEFORE anything swaps. It captures the janitor's live session, mints a session for the target via `admin.auth.admin.generateLink({type:'magiclink'})` (sends no email) + `verifyOtp({token_hash})`, and swaps the browser into it. The whole stack now runs as the member — their feed, settings, posts, gems — because the session genuinely IS theirs. Acting-as strips the janitor's own powers (they are now a member), which is the safe default; admin actions require Exit first.
2. **Exit restores the exact session; no escalation path.** The janitor's session (access+refresh tokens) is stashed in an httpOnly+secure cookie (`freq-act-as`, `lib/impersonation.ts`); `stopActingAsMember()` calls `setSession(stash)` to restore it verbatim and clears the cookie. Because Exit only ever restores the stashed tokens (never re-mints from an id), a forged cookie can only produce an INVALID session — never a session for an arbitrary user. A failsafe signs out (→ /sign-in) if restore fails, so the browser is never stranded as the member. The signout route also clears the cookie so a signout-while-acting-as can't leave a stale stash. Both enter and exit write an `impersonation.start` / `impersonation.stop` admin-audit row.
3. **A persistent, unmissable banner.** `components/layout/impersonation-banner.tsx` renders a sticky red "Acting as @X (you are <janitor>) · Exit" bar at the top of the app shell whenever the stash cookie is present — the only signal the session is borrowed, and the only Exit.
4. **Role-specific profile edit.** `profile.edit` (basic moderation: name/bio) is broadened from janitor-only to platform STAFF (admin OR janitor), so admins get the "Edit (mod)" popup. The profile page now shows: owner → "Edit profile"; staff → "Edit (mod)" + "Manage account"; janitor → additionally "Act as <name>" (gated on the real janitor web_role). `spotlight.manage` stays owner-or-janitor.

**Consequences.** New: `lib/impersonation.ts`, `app/(main)/impersonate-actions.ts`, `components/layout/impersonation-banner.tsx`. Changed: `lib/core/capabilities.ts` (profile.edit → staff; +test, 41 pass), `lib/auth.ts` (`getRealCallerWebRole`), `app/(main)/layout.tsx` (banner mount), `app/(main)/people/[handle]/page.tsx` (role-specific actions + Act-as), `app/auth/signout/route.ts` (clear stash). Guardrails: cannot act as the system account or yourself; account-delete + email-change are already member-inaccessible, so acting-as cannot reach them. Deferred / noted: a session swap can't be unit-tested without a live auth backend, so this is verified by the reviewer on the preview (Act as → banner → behaves as the member → Exit → back to janitor). Gate: tsc, eslint, authz-contract, vitest. Reversible: remove the Act-as button + the banner; the cookie is self-clearing on exit/signout.

---

## ADR-427: Delete functions for circles, practices, and events (+ existing journey delete)

**Status:** Accepted (2026-06-27). Circles and events gain a permanent Delete in their settings module; practices reuse the existing owner-or-admin delete (newly surfaced with a button); journeys already had delete. All gated on the entity's own manage capability, with a shared confirm control.
